event_blocks.summary: The performance begins with the 'EVERYMAN' logo on a black screen, accompanied by solemn choral singing, before transitioning to a dimly lit auditorium. As the choral music fades, the audience applauds a Man in Brown Suit, who stands center stage on a raised platform. He introduces himself as Dylan de Courfoudel, director of 'Everyman' and artistic director of the 'Gloria Historical Theater Society,' thanking the audience and acknowledging contributors, including Professor Lish McKenzie for the initial idea. He also thanks Dr. Nyman for 'Medieval Day 2016' support, Katie Lamb for makeup, Daniel Lance as musical director, Nicole Moraira as his 'partner in crime,' and Eric Hero for portraying 'Everyman.' Throughout his speech, the Man in Brown Suit gestures expressively, pointing and opening his arms. A Woman with a Lute briefly crosses the stage. The Stage Backdrop features three illuminated gothic arches with ivy, a small cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval ambiance. De Courfoudel describes the play as a 'medieval morality play,' 'super Christian' but with a meaning that 'transcends the universally Catholic nature of the Middle Ages.'

event_blocks.summary: The Man in Brown Suit, standing center stage, thanks the attentive Audience Members and gestures as he states, "Yes, thank you all very much, and please enjoy the show," while the Woman with Lute walks across the stage and exits. After a round of applause, the Man in Brown Suit also exits, leaving the stage momentarily empty before a melancholic lute melody begins, joined by a low, resonant male singing voice, against the backdrop of illuminated gothic arches. The Man in Red Tunic enters and appears to confront the Young Man in Beige Tunic, who is holding a mug. A Woman in White Tunic then enters from the right, wielding a long wooden staff, and engages the Man in Red in mock combat. The Man in Red draws a sword, and a distinct "clank" and "thud" sound as the Woman in White parries and dodges, ultimately disarming him. The Young Man observes the entire sequence as the two combatants stand facing each other, swords lowered, while the lute music and male singing continue.

event_blocks.summary: The scene opens on a stage with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, set to a somber, instrumental score. A Man in Red Tunic confronts a Young Man in Beige Tunic holding a mug, as a Woman in White Tunic enters with a long wooden staff. The Man in Red Tunic and the Woman in White Tunic then engage in a brief mock sword fight, accompanied by sounds of movement and clashing, while the Young Man in Beige Tunic observes. Subsequently, the Man in Red Tunic and the Young Man in Beige Tunic exit stage left, and the Woman in White Tunic exits stage right. A Woman in a long black robe over a white tunic enters from the center arch, holding a book. She proceeds to the center of the stage and, in a clear, theatrical female voice, begins reading aloud, "I pray you, all give your audience, and hear this matter with reverence. By figure, a moral play. The summoning of every man, called it is. That of our lives and ending shows, how transitory we be all day." She continues, "This matter is wonders precious, but the intent of it is more gracious, and swift to bear away. The story saith, man, in the beginning, look well and take good heed to thy ending, be ye never so gay." After closing the book and placing it on a small table, she removes her robe to reveal the white tunic underneath, and continues to speak expressively to the attentive audience, moving across the stage under focused lighting.

event_blocks.summary: The Woman in Black Robe, barefoot and clad in a long black robe over a white tunic, stood center stage on a raised platform before a backdrop of three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross, and a lantern. Reciting expressively with a clear, theatrical voice, she spoke of beginnings that lead to weeping, the body in clay, and how fellowship and jollity fade like a flower in May, before announcing a general reckoning called by the heaven king. She then gestured, turned, and exited through the right arch as medieval instrumental music with strings and light percussion began. Subsequently, the Woman in White Tunic, barefoot and wearing a dark tunic with a red sash and light-colored pants, entered confidently from the left. She walked across the stage, pausing to gesture and speak authoritatively to the audience about her majesty, how creatures are unkind, and people's blindness to her as their God, being drowned in sin and focused on worldly riches. Later, the Woman in White Tunic, again barefoot and in a dark tunic with a red sash and light-colored pants, stood center stage on the raised platform, delivering a dramatic, theatrical monologue in an archaic female voice. She used deliberate hand gestures and shifted her weight, speaking of morality, human failings, and judgment, with phrases like 'must in all the haste, have a reckoning of every man's person' and 'they will become much worse than beasts,' lamenting how people 'forsake me' and embrace 'seven deadly sins damnable' like 'pride, covetise, wrath, and lechery,' observing that 'the more that I then forbear, the worse they be from year to year,' as 'every man liveth so after his own pleasure.' She declared, 'Where art thou death, thou mighty messenger!' Towards the end of this segment, distinct footsteps were heard as the Woman in Black Robe, barefoot and in a long black robe over a white tunic, entered from the right, walked across the stage, turned, and exited through the right arch, briefly drawing attention before leaving the scene.

event_blocks.summary: On a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, a Woman in White Tunic, barefoot and speaking with a theatrical voice, commands an audience member to undertake an inescapable pilgrimage with a reckoning. She then exits, replaced by a Woman in Black Robe, also barefoot and holding a scythe upright, who declares her intention to search the world for those living outside God's laws, striking the rich with her dart unless alms be their friend, before she too exits. A Young Man in Beige Tunic enters, his footsteps audible, and exits. The Woman in Black Robe's voice continues off-stage, stating she sees every man walking. Later, the Woman in Black Robe, holding her scythe, speaks with a solemn, dramatic voice about coming for those focused on fleshly lusts and treasure, questioning the Young Man in Beige Tunic about forgetting his maker and his purpose. She reveals she is sent by God, prompting the Young Man's surprised "What? Sent to me?" She confirms this, adding, "here we depart, ye shall know," and that a reckoning is required without respite. The Young Man pleads for more time, but the Woman declares, "On thee, thou must take a long journey." The scene shifts with the Woman in Black Robe stating, "Return again, thou cannot, by no way," accusing the Young Man of many bad deeds and few good ones, explaining her role before the "chief Lord of Paradise." The Young Man, unready and fearful, asks her identity, to which she reveals, "I am Death," explaining it is God's commandment that all obey her. The Young Man, resigned, acknowledges her power, and she offers a conditional reprieve for "a thousand pounds." A melancholic string instrument plays as the Young Man pleads for more time, but the Woman refuses bribes, stating her "custom is clean" and commanding him to come. He cries, "Alas, shall I have no respite?" reflecting on death giving no warning and his fear, begging for "twelve year" more. The Woman dismisses his pleas, stating it's "Be avail, not to cry, weep, or pray," and commands him to "hastily that thou wert gone this journey," suggesting he "prove thy friends." The Woman then states, "with indeed thou can. For with thou well, I divine it to no man. And in this world, each living creature, for Adam's sin must die, of nature." The Young Man cries out to God for mercy, asking if he will have company. The Woman sarcastically asks if anyone would be hardy enough to go with him, telling him to give his reckoning before the Lord. The Young Man picks up a wooden staff, mirroring the scythe, and gestures. Confused, he asks, "What, oh, what..." The Woman challenges if he thought his life and goods were given, to which he replies, "I had weened so verily." She dismisses this, "Nay, nay, 'twas but lent thee." Finally, the Woman in Black Robe declares, "After thou art gone, another for a while shall have it... for suddenly, I do come." The Young Man, distressed, asks, "Oh wretched caitiff, whither shall I flee? Shall I have no respite? ... shall I not be saved?" The Woman states, "They, thereto, I shall not consent, nor any man shall I respite. And yet swiftly into the heart I shall smite... for thou mayest say that no living man may escape this name." The Young Man uses the staff to gesture, and as mournful string music plays, he despairs, "Alas, I may well weep with sighs deep." Throughout these interactions, Audience Members watch attentively, their heads occasionally turning to follow the dramatic confrontations, while the focused stage lighting highlights the performers and the symbolic backdrop.

The Woman in Black Robe shifts her stance to emphasize her authoritative presence, continuing her pronouncements: "They, thereto, I shall not consent, nor any man shall I respite. And yet swiftly into the heart I shall smite... for thou mayest say that no living man may escape this name." The Young Man then picks up a wooden staff, using it in his gestures, suggesting a shift from pleading to confrontation. At one point, he turns and walks toward the center, while the Woman in Black Robe retreats slightly, then re-enters from the left, continuing the interaction. As solemn, mournful string music begins to play, the Young Man's voice returns, filled with despair, "Alas, I may well weep with sighs deep." The audience, seated in the foreground, watches attentively, their heads occasionally turning to follow the action, as the lighting remains focused on the stage, highlighting the performers and the symbolic backdrop, and the overall atmosphere grows increasingly dramatic.

event_blocks.summary: On a stage adorned with illuminated gothic arches, ivy, a cross, and a lantern, a Man in Beige Tunic, holding a wooden staff, laments his lack of company for a journey, questioning how he will manage and expressing regret for embarking. A Young Man in Beige Tunic dramatically enters, sits, and drinks from a dark object, while the first man, shifting to hope, declares his intention to speak with him. The Man in Beige Tunic greets the Young Man, noting his piteous state and offering help, to which the Young Man responds with distress about his jeopardy. Reassured by the Man's promise of loyalty, the Young Man expresses his fear of being abandoned, but the Man in Beige Tunic vows protection, dismissing thanks and urging him to share his grief. The Man in Beige Tunic then explains his need to embark on a dangerous journey to give a reckoning and asks the Young Man to accompany him, culminating in a dramatic embrace and the Young Man's wailing. Later, the Man in Beige Tunic questions a 'voyage' and states he will not go if faith is lacking, while the Young Man asserts Death was with him and reminds the Man of his promise not to forsake him. The Man in Beige Tunic argues against going on such a journey if Death is the messenger, but the Young Man insists the Man's mind is more inclined to mirth than accompanying him. The Man in Beige Tunic then urges listeners to 'eat and drink and make good cheer,' but pleads for help in his necessity, declaring he will not go with 'Fellowship.' Fellowship, agitated, rejects him, stating he will depart, and the Man in Beige Tunic, lamenting being forsaken, asks where Fellowship is going. Fellowship confirms his departure, and the Man in Beige Tunic bids him farewell, his heart sore. The Man in Beige Tunic then delivers a sorrowful monologue about parting and being forsaken, before the Man in Red Tunic and a Woman in White Tunic enter. The Man in Beige Tunic turns to greet them, asking where his friends and kinsmen are. He then commands, 'Show us your intent,' and declares, 'we will live and die together,' speaking of 'wealth and woe,' while the Man in Red Tunic promises to 'show you the grief of my mind.' The Man in Beige Tunic explains he is commanded by a messenger to go on a pilgrimage to his pain, knowing he will never return and must give a reckoning due to a great enemy. The Woman in White Tunic interjects, asking what account must be rendered, and the Man in Red Tunic replies, 'Of all my works, I must show how I have lived.' Later, the Man in Beige Tunic pleads for someone to 'come with me to make my reckoning, for charity?' A Woman in White Tunic questions going with 'her,' stating she has lived with fast bread and water and warns he will go alone. Another woman, the Woman in Brown Cloak, refuses to go, citing a cramp in her toe and warning of deception. The Woman in White Tunic offers her maid, but the Man in Beige Tunic asks if they will abide behind. The Woman in Brown Cloak confirms she will abide, bidding farewell. The Man in Beige Tunic laments being deceived and sad, then takes the Woman in Brown Cloak’s staff, helps her put on his hat, and she dons his cloak, delivering a farewell before exiting. The Man in Beige Tunic is left alone center stage, holding his staff, as the audience watches attentively throughout.

raw_segments: The scene opens on a raised platform where a man, dressed as a pilgrim in a beige tunic, light pants, and a wide-brimmed hat, stands center stage holding a wooden staff. He delivers an expressive monologue to the attentive audience, his theatrical male voice lamenting, "Now have I no manner of company to help me in my journey," and questioning, "How shall I do now for to excuse me?" He continues, "I would to God I had never been got," and asks, "To whom were I best my complaint to make?" as he gestures and occasionally adjusts his hat and staff. A distinct thump sound is heard. He then turns and gestures left, prompting a second man, also in a beige tunic and brown pants, barefoot with a black belt, to make a dramatic, theatrical entrance from the left. This young man bends low before sitting on the platform's edge in a relaxed, almost defiant pose, holding a dark object, possibly a mug, from which he drinks while looking toward the first man. The first man continues his speech, his tone shifting to hope, declaring, "I seek him yonder certainly, I trust that he will bear me company. Therefore to him will I speak to ease my soul." He points and raises his staff, directly addressing the seated man with a welcoming, "Well met good fellowship." The audience watches intently throughout the interaction, framed by the illuminated gothic arches of the medieval-style backdrop.

The scene unfolds on a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. Two men are present: the Man in Beige Tunic, standing center stage on a raised platform, holding a wooden staff, and the Young Man in Beige Tunic, barefoot, who enters dramatically from the left. The Man in Beige Tunic, speaking with a theatrical, older-sounding voice, greets the other, saying, "Oh, my man! Good morrow by this day," and observes, "But lookest thou so piteously." He gestures expressively, occasionally adjusting his wide-brimmed hat and pointing, offering help: "If anything be amiss, I pray thee be safe, so I may help to remedy." The Young Man in Beige Tunic, after his entrance, sits on the edge of the platform, holding a dark object and drinking from it. He responds with a younger, slightly distressed voice, declaring, "I am in great jeopardy." The Man in Beige Tunic reassures him, "My true friend, show me to your mind. I would not forsake thee, it's my life's act, in the way of good company." The Young Man, gesturing, appreciates this, stating, "That was well spoken and lovingly, sir." He then expresses his distress further: "If my heart should to you break, and you turn your mind from me, and not me comfort when ye hear me speak, then ten times sorry or I should be." The Man in Beige Tunic continues to speak animatedly, promising protection: "If anything have you wrong... then she shall be, though I have the ground be slain for thee, and before that I know that I should die." He dismisses the Young Man's thanks ("Tush! By thy thanks, I set not a straw") and urges him to "Show me your grief and say no more." The Young Man concludes his plea with "Sir, I say, as I will do indeed." Throughout this dynamic interaction, the audience, seated in the foreground, watches attentively, their heads occasionally shifting to follow the action. The consistent lighting highlights the performers against the dark backdrop, maintaining a theatrical and engaging atmosphere. No background music or environmental sounds are present, focusing entirely on the dramatic dialogue.

The scene unfolds on a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval ambiance, underscored by medieval-style string music. The Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a tunic, light pants, and a wide-brimmed hat, stands center stage, holding a wooden staff. He speaks with a deep, theatrical voice, declaring his loyalty and friendship. The Young Man in Beige Tunic, similarly dressed but barefoot, enters dramatically from the left, approaching the Man. He responds with a higher-pitched theatrical voice, acknowledging the Man's words. Their dialogue continues, with the Man in Beige Tunic explaining his need to embark on a "long, dangerous journey" to "give a reckoning straight," and asking the Young Man to accompany him. Throughout this, the Man uses expressive gestures, raising his staff and adjusting his hat. The Young Man drinks from a dark object he holds, and their interaction involves playful movements. As the Man finishes his plea, he places his arm around the Young Man, who then leans back dramatically and emits a loud, dramatic wailing or crying sound, followed by a deep sigh, before regaining his composure to state, "That is better indeed. Promise is duty, but..." The audience, seated in the foreground, watches attentively, their heads occasionally shifting to follow the action on stage, which remains consistently lit.

The scene unfolds on a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. Two men engage in a dramatic, theatrical dialogue. The Man in Beige Tunic, wearing a wide-brimmed hat and holding a wooden staff, speaks with a formal, cautious tone, questioning a 'voyage' and stating, "I know it well, it should be to my pain," and later, "If faith I will not that way go." He gestures expressively, adjusts his hat, and clasps his hands. The Young Man in Beige Tunic, barefoot and holding a dark object from which he drinks, responds with an assertive, challenging tone, reminding the other, "Ye said if I had need, ye would never forsake me," and dramatically declares, "Indeed, Death was with me here." He points and moves animatedly across the stage. Their intense conversation continues, with the Man in Beige Tunic further debating, "If Death be the messenger, for no man living today would I set foot on that loath journey," and the Young Man asserting, "Your mind will sooner apply to go to mirth and solace and play, than to bear me company in my long journey." The audience watches attentively, their heads occasionally shifting to follow the performers under consistent, focused lighting.

The scene unfolds on a stage framed by three illuminated gothic-style arches adorned with green ivy, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. A Man in Beige Tunic, wearing a wide-brimmed hat and holding a wooden staff, stands center stage, speaking expressively with a theatrical male voice, urging listeners to 'eat and drink and make good cheer,' and to 'trust me, verily.' A Young Man in Beige Tunic, barefoot and dressed similarly, enters from the right, holding a dark object resembling a mug or cup from which he drinks. He responds to the Man in Beige Tunic with a distinct, slightly more refined male voice, remarking, 'Oh, that is simply enticing indeed.' The Man in Beige Tunic then pleads, 'Now, gentle fellow, help me in my necessity... Now, gentle fellowship, remember me,' his voice growing urgent as he declares, 'by Saint John I will not with thee go!' The Young Man, now explicitly addressed as 'Fellowship,' replies, 'Yet I pray thee, take the labor and do so much for me,' his tone somewhat dismissive. The Man in Beige Tunic desperately calls out, 'Fellowship, grant mercy.' Fellowship, becoming agitated, rejects him, stating, 'Nay, if thou hadst not tarried, I would not have forsaken thee so. So now, as God speed thee on thy journey, I will depart from thee, as fast as I may.' The Man in Beige Tunic pleads again, 'Whither away, Fellowship? Will ye forsake me?' to which Fellowship confirms, 'Yea, by my faith, to God I betake thee.' Fellowship then delivers a somber farewell, 'Farewell, good Fellowship, for this my heart is sore,' as he continues to interact dynamically, moving between the arches and occasionally addressing the attentive audience. The lighting remains focused on the performers, highlighting their dramatic interaction against the dark backdrop, suggesting a theatrical performance with a spiritual or historical theme.

The Man in Beige Tunic continues his somber speech, asking, "Alack? Shall we thus depart indeed? O lady, help without any more comfort," and lamenting, "fellowship forsaketh me in my most need." Sit that fellowship hath forsaken me," and later, "To my kinsmen, I will truly, pray them to help me in my necessity." The Man in Red Tunic and the Woman in White Tunic then enter from the right, walking toward the center. The Man in Beige Tunic turns to greet them, gesturing toward them as he concludes his speech, "Where be ye now, my friends and kinsmen, lo?" On a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval or religious ambiance, three performers engage in a dramatic scene. A man in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, and a wide-brimmed hat, holding a wooden staff, speaks expressively with a dramatic male voice, gesturing and occasionally adjusting his hat. He recounts his 'days spent' and 'ill deeds,' then speaks of 'all virtues I have profused,' pleading for someone to 'come with me to make my reckoning,' asking, 'For charity?' A woman in a white tunic with a red vest and brown pants, barefoot and holding a long staff, turns to interact, responding with a clear female voice, questioning, 'What? To go with her? Is that the matter?' She continues, stating, 'Nay, every man, I have lived with fast bread and water all this five year and more,' and later warns him, 'ye shall go alone.' The man then asks, 'My cousin, will you not with me go?' to which another woman in a white tunic with a brown cloak, also barefoot and holding a staff, responds with a distinct female voice, refusing, 'No, by our lady, I have a cramp in my toe,' and further warns, 'Trust not to me, for so God me speed, I will deceive you in your most need.' The first woman then adds, 'If it avail not us to please. Ye shall have my maid, with all my...' On a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, three performers engage in a dramatic scene. The Woman in White Tunic, in a white tunic with a red vest, brown pants, and holding a long staff, turns to the Woman in Brown Cloak (white tunic, brown cloak, barefoot, staff) and speaks in a clear female voice, stating, "She loves to go to feast, there to be nice, and to dance abroad to start. I will give her leave to join you in that journey, if that you and she may agree." The Man in Beige Tunic (wide-brimmed hat, beige tunic, wooden staff) observes, then responds in a male voice, asking, "No, show me the very effect of your mind. Will you not go with me or abide behind?" The Woman in Brown Cloak replies in a distinct female voice, "Abide behind. Yea, that will I, and I may. Therefore, farewell, till another day." Following this exchange, the Woman in White Tunic exits stage right. The Man in Beige Tunic and Woman in Brown Cloak continue their interaction. The Man in Beige Tunic then speaks expressively in a male voice, lamenting, "Alas, shall I never be merry or glad? For fair promises meant to be make, yet when I have need, they me forsake. I am deceived, that maketh me sad." During his speech, he gestures and adjusts his hat. A distinct "clunk" sound is heard as he places his staff on the ground, followed by a brief, dramatic laugh. He then takes the Woman in Brown Cloak’s staff, helps her put on his hat, and she dons his cloak. The Woman in Brown Cloak, now wearing the Man's hat and cloak, delivers a farewell in her distinct female voice, saying, "Cousin every man, farewell now, for verily I will not go with you... Now God keep thee for now I go." After her speech, she exits stage right, leaving the Man in Beige Tunic alone center stage, holding his staff. The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, their heads occasionally shifting to follow the action. The lighting remains consistent, focused on the performers, enhancing the dramatic tone of the scene.

event_blocks.summary: On a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a Man in Beige Tunic, holding a wooden staff and wearing a wide-brimmed hat, laments his past deceptions and misplaced love for worldly goods, stating, "Oh goods. Thou hast had long my hearty love, which should have been given to the Lord above." He questions if his goods will accompany him on his journey, to which the Man in Red Tunic, entering from the right with a mug-like object, responds with a definitive, "No, so God me speed. Therefore, farewell, and have a good day." The Man in Beige Tunic then descends from a raised platform, sits on a black box, and continues his monologue, recounting how fellowship and kinsmen abandoned him, and his "goods" proved fruitless. Melancholic, lute-like music begins as he reflects on his wasted time and the need to seek counsel, eventually declaring, "I think that I shall never speed, till that I go to my good deeds." He exits through an arch, re-enters holding his hat and staff, and calls out, "My good deeds, where be you?" accompanied by rhythmic tapping sounds, possibly from his staff, as the audience watches attentively.

raw_segments: The scene opens with the Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, and a wide-brimmed hat, holding a wooden staff, standing center stage on a raised platform. He speaks expressively to the attentive audience, gesturing with his hands and occasionally adjusting his hat, against a backdrop of three illuminated gothic-style arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross, and a lantern, while melancholic, medieval-style instrumental music plays softly. He delivers a theatrical monologue with a mournful and dramatic tone, lamenting, "Ah, Jesu, is all come here to love fair words make fools me. They promise and nothing will do certain." He continues, "My kinsmen promised me faithfully to abide by me steadfastly. Yet fast away do they flee. Even so, fellowship promised me," his voice conveying a sense of betrayal. He then shifts, stating, "Yet, in my mind a thing there is. All my life I have loved riches. If that my good would help me might, it would make my heart full light. I will speak to him in this distress," before dramatically asking, "Where art thou, my goods and riches?" Around the 50-second mark, the Man in Red Tunic, wearing a red tunic, green vest, and red cap, enters from the right, holding a dark object resembling a mug or cup. He walks toward the center stage, gesturing expressively as he approaches the Man in Beige Tunic, and with a deeper, more resonant voice, questions, "Who calleth me? Everyman? What haste hast thou?" initiating an interaction between the two performers as the music continues.

The scene unfolds on a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, creating a medieval or religious ambiance, further enhanced by a small cross on the left arch and a lantern on the right. A Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, a wide-brimmed hat, and a brown cloak, stands barefoot center stage, holding a wooden staff. A consistent, melancholic, and dramatic background music with string instruments plays softly. To his left, a Man in Red Tunic, wearing a red tunic, green vest, and red cap, enters from the right, approaching the Man in Beige Tunic while holding a dark object resembling a mug or cup. The Man in Red Tunic, with a distinct, theatrical, and slightly urgent voice, interjects, asking, "What will he have?" The Man in Beige Tunic then invites him closer for "counsel." The two engage in animated conversation, with the Man in Red Tunic gesturing emphatically and moving between the arches, offering to remedy "sorrow or adversity" but then clarifying that "It is another disease that grieveth me." The Man in Beige Tunic responds, stating, "It is not of this world, I tell thee so," and continues to speak of being "sent for another way to go" to give an account before "the highest Jupiter of all." He then expresses, "And all my life, I have loved thee," before imploring the Man in Red Tunic to "go with me" to "help clean and purify" his "reckoning," concluding with a proverb about money. He walks toward the center, engaging in a dynamic conversation with the Man in Beige Tunic. The Man in Red Tunic speaks with a theatrical, urgent, and passionate voice, expressing, "Alas, I should ever be afraid if I come to that fearful answer without thee," and urging, "Yea, come thither up and let's go together." The Man in Beige Tunic responds, "Nay, I am too personal," as the two interact with a mix of rapid dialogue and physical movement, including moments where the Man in Red Tunic gestures widely and the Man in Beige Tunic responds with open-handed gestures and slight turns, even adjusting his hat. The Man in Red Tunic continues his passionate speech, lamenting, "Alas, I have thee loved all my life and have had great pleasure in my goods and treasure," and further explaining how his love is "contrary to the love everlasting," conveying regret and dramatic emotion. He continues, reflecting on a past "season, found in happy and prosperity," and stating, "My condition is man so to kill. If I save one, how should I just spill?" He speaks of following a "style... not from this world, verily," having "weened otherwise." His monologue grows in intensity as he proclaims, "Therefore, to thy soul, curses a thief, oh, when thou art dead. This is my guidance. Another to deceive and to save wise, as I had done, and all thy soul grieves." With heightened emotion, he exclaims, "Oh, false good! Cursed may thou be! Thou traitor to God! Thou hast deceived me! And caught me in thy snare." During this, the Man in Red Tunic, wearing a red tunic, green vest, and red cap, enters from the right, walks toward the center, and engages in animated conversation with the Man in Beige Tunic, gesturing expressively as he moves between the arches, holding a dark object resembling a mug or cup. The Man in Beige Tunic concludes his impassioned speech, "Mary, thou broughtest thyself in care, where I am right glad, I must be glad. I cannot be sad." The audience, seated in the foreground, watches attentively, their heads occasionally shifting to follow the performers' movements, as the consistent lighting highlights the theatrical and engaging atmosphere.

The scene opens on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. His words were very pleasant and gay, but afterward, he left me alone." He further recounts, "Then spake I to my kinsmen, all in despair. They also gave me words fair. They lacked no fair speaking, but all forsake me in the ending." His narrative continues, expressing a search for comfort from his "goods," which also proved fruitless. The audience, seated in the foreground, watches attentively, their heads occasionally shifting to follow the engaging, theatrical performance, highlighted by the focused stage lighting.

The scene opens with a man, dressed in a beige tunic, brown pants, and a long brown cloak, seated barefoot on a black box center stage, holding a wooden staff. He wears a wide-brimmed hat, which he adjusts as he speaks expressively to the attentive audience, framed by three illuminated gothic-style arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. He delivers lines like, "For my goods, sharply did me tell, that he bring it many in hell," and continues, "And so was I ashamed. So I am worthy to be blamed. And so I may well myself hate. Of whom counsel shall I now take?" The man gestures with his hands and staff, then stands up, walking across the stage while declaring, "I think that I shall never speed, till that I go to my good deeds." A distinct thud is heard, possibly from his staff. He exits through the right arch, re-entering shortly after, now holding his hat in one hand and the staff in the other, continuing to gesture and speak with emphasis, asking, "My good deeds, where be you?" Rhythmic tapping sounds, likely from his staff, accompany his movements as he moves toward the center, turns, and briefly disappears behind a small gray box before re-emerging. A melancholic, medieval-style melody with string and flute instruments begins to play in the background, accompanying his continued performance as the audience remains engaged.

event_blocks.summary: On a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches, a cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval ambiance underscored by melancholic music, a barefoot woman in a black robe, initially distressed and crouched behind a black box, laments her sins. A barefoot man in a beige tunic and wide-brimmed hat, holding a staff, enters and urgently seeks her counsel, gesturing expressively as he helps her to her feet and then kneels before her. The woman, now seated on the box, states he is summoned to account, and she will journey with him. He expresses a desire to go together, but she claims she cannot stand. He questions why, and she implies his responsibility for her predicament. Later, the woman in black, seated calmly on the box with a book, tells the kneeling man that his book of accounts is ready and shows him the weight of his deeds. The man, holding a dark book, cries out for help as he cannot read it. The woman observes his 'blind reckoning,' and he desperately pleads for help from good deeds to avoid damnation. The woman, addressing him as 'Everyman,' expresses sorrow for his fall and offers conditional help through her sister, Knowledge. The man pleads for counsel from his good deeds. The woman agrees to send Knowledge, stating she cannot go herself. The scene shifts with the man in beige tunic engaging in animated conversation with the woman in black, who promises to be his guide. To her right, a woman in a white tunic and brown cloak, also barefoot and holding a staff, questions who will heal him after his reckoning and deeds. The woman in black instructs them to go to confession, and the man, weeping for joy, asks where confession resides. The woman in the brown cloak directs them to the 'house of salvation.' Throughout, the audience watches attentively, with one person recording on a phone, under consistent lighting that maintains a solemn and dramatic tone.

raw_segments: The scene unfolds on a stage bathed in focused light, featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance, underscored by melancholic medieval-style background music. A woman in a black robe, barefoot and holding a scythe, is initially crouched behind a black box, appearing distressed as she speaks in a solemn voice, lamenting, "Here I lie, cold in the ground. My sins have me so sore bound that I cannot stir." A man in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, and a wide-brimmed hat, also barefoot and holding a wooden staff, enters from the right. He approaches the woman, speaking with an urgent and dramatic tone, seeking counsel: "Oh, good deeds, I stand in great fear. I must desire of your counsel, for help now should come right well." He gestures expressively, gently helps her to her feet, and then kneels before her, continuing to speak with animated hand gestures as the background music persists. The woman, now seated on the box and holding a small object, looks down and addresses him, stating, "Everyman, I have understanding that thou art summoned account to make. For Messiahs of Jerusalem came. And you do by me, that journey with you will I take." The man responds, "Therefore, I come to you my moan to make. I pray thee, let's go thither together." The woman replies, "I would full fain, but I cannot stand verily," to which the man questions, "Why? Is there anything on you fall?" She concludes, "Yea, sir, I may thank you of all," implying his involvement in her predicament. The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person recording the scene on a phone, emphasizing the spiritual or emotional significance of the interaction.

The scene unfolds on a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross on the left arch, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person holding up a phone to record the performance. The atmosphere is solemn and theatrical as the Woman in Black Robe, seated calmly on a black box, holding a book in her lap, addresses the Man in Beige Tunic, who kneels before her. She speaks with a calm, authoritative tone, stating, "If ye have perfectly cheered me, your book of accounts full ready now with thee," and then, "Look, the books of your works and deeds. Behold how they lie under the feet to your soul's heaviness." The Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a beige tunic and barefoot, gestures expressively with his hands, holding a dark book, and pleads with urgency and distress, crying out, "Our Lord Jesus, help me! For one letter herein can I not see!" The Woman in Black Robe responds with a calm, almost cold observation, "Here is a blind reckoning in time of distress." The man, now more desperate, exclaims, "Oh good deeds, help me in this need, or else I am forever damned indeed! Help me to make my reckoning before the redeemer of all thing that can is and was and ever shall." The Woman in Black Robe, addressing him as "Everyman," softens her tone slightly, expressing, "I am sorry of your fall, and fain would I help you, and I were able." The Man in Beige Tunic continues to plead earnestly, "My good deeds, your counsel, I pray you, give me." Finally, the Woman in Black Robe offers conditional assistance, stating, "That shall I do, verily. Though that on my feet I may not go, I have a sister that shall with you also, called Knowledge, that shall with you abide to help you make that..." Her voice trails off as the minute concludes, leaving the man's plea hanging in the air.

The scene unfolds on a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, establishing a medieval or religious ambiance. Center stage, a woman in a long black robe over a white tunic, barefoot and seated on a black box, holds a book or tablet and listens intently. She speaks with a clear, guiding voice, declaring, "Every man, I will go with thee and be thy guide in thy most need to go by thy side." To her left, a man in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, and barefoot, stands holding a staff and engages in animated conversation, gesturing expressively as he speaks with urgency and reverence, stating, "In good condition, I am now in every day. I thank my goods. God help. I thank God, my creature." To her right, a woman in a white tunic with a brown cloak, also barefoot and holding a staff, stands and responds with subtle gestures, turning her head to engage. She questions, "And when he hath brought thee there, who will to heal thee of thy smart? Though thou with thy reckoning and good deeds gather, who will make thee joyful at the heart before the blessed Trinity?" As medieval-style music begins to play, the woman in black robe instructs, "Now, go we together, lovingly, to confession, that cleansing river." The man in the beige tunic moves between the arches, at one point kneeling before the seated woman, expressing emotion and asking, "I weep for joy. I wish there were, but I must pray you to instruct me by your direction. Where dwelleth that holy virtue, confession?" The woman in the brown cloak then answers, "In the house of salvation, we shall find him in that place. Then, show us comfort." The man rises to continue the dialogue, and the woman in the brown cloak shifts her position slightly, turning toward him. The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person holding up a phone to record, all under consistent lighting that emphasizes the solemn and dramatic tone.

event_blocks.summary: On a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches, ivy, a cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval ambiance accompanied by solemn instrumental music, an audience observes a ritual. A woman in a dark dress with a red sash and cross necklace gestures towards a kneeling man in a brown robe and white hat. This man kneels before a seated woman in a black robe, who holds a book/tablet and a scythe. The seated woman, speaking with an authoritative, solemn voice, initiates the confession, stating, "By God's grace. Lo, this is confession. Kneel down and ask mercy. For he is in good to see with God Almighty." The kneeling man responds with a reverent, pleading male voice, seeking absolution: "Oh blessed fountainhead, that all uncleanness doth clarify. Wash from me the spots of vices and unclean. That on me no sin may be seen. Oh, shriven mother of salvation, repent with hearts of true contrition. Spare me now of my sins." The seated woman, with subtle hand movements, acknowledges his sorrow and offers guidance: "I know your sorrow well, every man. Because with knowledge you come to me. I will you comfort as well as I can. And a precious jewel I will give you. Call penance, water of adversity. Therewith shall your body chastise me. Abstinence and perseverance in God's service." Later, a man in a beige tunic kneels before the seated woman in black, who holds a staff. He declares with a clear, theatrical male voice, "Here which shall you receive that scourge from me, which is strong, you must endure. Remember thy savior, who scourged for thee, and sharp sharp scourges, and suffered patiently. So must thou, ere thou pass thy pilgrimage." He continues, urging, "Knowledge, keep him in this voyage, and by that time good deeds shall be with thee. But in any wise, be sure of mercy. Your time draws fast, but he will save it thee. Ask God mercy, and he will grant true. When with the scourge of penance, man doth combine, the oil of forgiveness they shall be found." During his speech, he gestures expressively. A woman in a dark dress with a red sash gestures towards him, and another woman in a black robe stands behind the seated figure. The kneeling man then rises, removes his white hat, and walks forward between the arches, stating, "Obviously, Jesu, all there speak. For now I am ready of penance, God, we are guide." The seated woman, holding a book and scythe, responds with an authoritative female voice, "Every man, look your penance that ye fulfill, what pain that ever see thee. And I will give you counsel at will, how your account ye shall make clearly." As the man rises, the standing woman in black steps forward, while the woman in the dark dress exits the stage, as the audience, including one person recording, watches.

event_blocks.summary: On a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval or religious ambiance, a solemn male liturgical chanting begins and continues. The Man in Beige Tunic, initially in a beige tunic and brown cloak, interacts with the Woman in Black Robe (Standing), who holds a book. He then removes his cloak, revealing a white tunic with a black belt, clasps his hands in prayer, and kneels, declaring in a clear, resonant voice against the chanting, "In thy hands, my soul, I commend. Receive it, Lord, that it be not lost. To save me from the fiend's boast. That I may be with that blessed host, that shall be saved by the day of doom." He then recites in Latin, "In manus tuas, of might most, forever commendo spiritum meum." Maintaining his prayerful pose, he collapses forward onto the stage floor, lying motionless. The Woman in Black Robe (Standing) steps back slightly, while the Woman in Black Robe (Seated), who had been observing from a black box, rises and moves toward the fallen man. On the left, a woman in a brown cloak and white hat, holding a book, recites, "Now, hath he suffered that which we all shall endure. Though good deeds shall make all sure." As the Man in Beige Tunic lies motionless on a raised platform, dressed in a white tunic and beige pants, barefoot, appearing deceased, the woman in the black robe approaches him from the right, drapes a white cloth over his body, and retrieves a scythe. As she holds the scythe upright, the woman in the brown cloak steps forward, placing her hand on its handle, continuing her recitation, "Now, hath he made ending, me thinketh I hear angels sing. And make great joy and melody, where every man's soul shall received be." She further proclaims, "Now come, excellent, let's suppose to Jesse, you're about thou shalt go. Now that thy soul has taken thy body pro, thy reckoning is crystal clear. And thou shalt into the heavenly sphere, unto which all ye that live well, shalt go after the day of doom." The audience watches intently, with one person recording on a phone, as the dramatic and ritualistic performance is emphasized by the focused stage lighting and the evocative, continuous chant.

Center stage, a barefoot man in a beige tunic and brown cloak kneels, holding a white hat. He rises, gesturing expressively as he speaks with profound reverence, reciting a prayer-like plea to 'Oh, eternal God, oh heavenly figure... a way of right wiseness, oh vision pure,' asking for redemption from his sins 'that I may not go to purgatory.' He continues, requesting 'Knowledge, give me the scourge of pens.' To his right, a barefoot woman in a black robe, seated solemnly on a black box, holding a book and scythe, listens intently. She responds with an authoritative voice, stating, 'Everyman, God give you time and space. Thus, I bequeath you in the hands of our savior. Now, may you make your reckoning sure.' Behind her, another woman in a black robe stands, observing the interaction with a solemn expression. The man, seemingly bewildered, exclaims, 'What? I weened this were an allegory!' This elicits laughter from the attentive audience, one member recording the performance on a phone, as the background music continues.

The scene unfolds on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. Center stage, the Man in Beige Tunic, barefoot and cloaked, holds a white hat, speaking with a deep, resonant male voice and profound reverence, declaring, "In the name of all the Holy Trinity, my body punished sore shall be." His expressive gestures are punctuated by distinct thudding sounds as he continues, "Take this body for the sin of the flesh, and also thou delightest to go gay and fresh," and later, "Now, my purgatory I will wait the water clear, as to avoid that purgatory, that sharp fire." To his right, the Woman in Black Robe (Seated), barefoot and robed, holding a book and scythe, listens intently with a solemn expression. She then rises, moving to stand beside the Man, and speaks with a clear female voice, expressing gratitude: "I thank God now I can walk and go, and am healed of my sickness and woe. Therefore, with every man I will go, and not spare. His good works I will help him to declare." To the far right, the Woman in Black Robe (Standing), also robed and barefoot, holding a book, observes with a composed demeanor before stepping forward slightly, gesturing subtly, and engaging in the conversation with a distinct female voice, proclaiming, "Now, every man, be merry and glad, your good deeds cometh now, ye may not be sad. Now is your good deeds whole and sound." Throughout, the audience watches attentively, with one person recording on a phone, as consistent lighting highlights the performers against the dark backdrop, maintaining the dramatic and contemplative atmosphere.

The scene unfolds on a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. Center stage, a barefoot man in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, and a brown cloak, holding a white hat, begins by gesturing expressively. A clear female voice, likely from the woman standing to his left, initiates the dialogue, stating, "Going upright upon the ground." The man then kneels before a black table, picks up the hat, and rises, declaring in a resonant voice, "My heart is light and shall be evermore. Now will I smite faster than I did before." To his left, a woman in a dark robe with a white tunic and a cross necklace stands holding a book, observing solemnly. To his right, a barefoot woman in a black robe over a white tunic, seated on a black box, holds a book and a scythe, maintaining a contemplative presence. The female voice continues, addressing the man as "pilgrim, my special friend," and blessing him, adding, "For these prepared the eternal glory. Ye have made me whole and sound. Therefore, I will abide by thee in every stand." The man interacts with both women, turning to each as he speaks, bringing his hands together in a prayer-like motion. He responds, "Oh, welcome good deeds, for now I hear thy voice," and then asks, "Have we clear our reckoning?" The female voice replies, "Yay, indeed. I have here." The man then concludes, "Excellent. Then friends, I think we need not tarry." As he rises, the woman on the right steps forward slightly, while the woman on the left gestures subtly while holding her book. The female voice continues to speak, advising, "Be no more sad, but evermore rejoice. God seeth thy living in his throne above," and then instructs, "Put on this garment to thy beho, with which your tears is now all wet, lest before God it be unswept when ye to your journey's end shall." The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person recording the performance on a phone, all under consistent lighting that enhances the dramatic and solemn tone.

The scene unfolds on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. A man in a beige tunic, barefoot, holding a white hat and a staff, stands center stage, speaking expressively and gesturing with his hands, beginning with the words, "ye it call." To his left, a woman in a dark robe, barefoot, holds a book or tablet and gestures subtly, then speaks with a formal, slightly higher-pitched voice, stating, "It is the garment of sorrow. From pain it will you borrow. Contrition it is, that getteth forgiveness. It pleaseth God passing well." She continues, asking, "Every man, will you wear it for your health?" The man in the beige tunic replies, "Yea, verily." He then addresses the group, saying, "Now friends, I think we meet not to depart in twain." The woman with the book responds, "Nay every man, that we will not sustain." Solemn, medieval-style string music, reminiscent of a lute or harp, begins to play. The man then turns and walks toward the right arch, followed by another woman in a black robe who steps forward slightly. He exits through the right arch, then re-enters from the left, now wearing the white hat, and rejoins the group. The woman on the left, holding the book, gestures toward him as he returns. The three stand together again, continuing their interaction with subtle movements and expressions, maintaining a solemn and reverent tone. The woman with the book advises, "Yet must thou leave with thee three persons of great might." The man asks, "Who should they be?" She replies, "Strength and discretion, they have, and your beauty may not abide." A light crashing sound is heard. She continues, "Ye must also call to mind, your five wits, as for your counselors. You must have them ready at all hours." The man questions, "How shall I get them hither?" and she concludes, "Ye must call them all together, and they will hear you." The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person holding up a phone to record the performance.

The scene unfolds on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, establishing a medieval or religious ambiance, underscored by the gentle plucking of a string instrument. Initially, a man in a beige tunic stands center stage, speaking expressively with a clear, theatrical male voice, declaring, "My friends, I call you all here to be present," and listing qualities like "discretion, strength, my five wits and beauty." He gestures, occasionally bringing his hands together in a prayer-like motion. A distinct, higher-pitched string melody plays, followed by audible laughter from the attentive audience, one member recording on a phone. A woman in a white tunic, holding a long staff, then speaks with a clear female voice, asking, "Here at your will, we all ready. What would ye do...?" and "will you go with me or not in that voyage?" From the right, a group of four new characters enters, including a young man in a beige tunic who, holding a dark mug, responds with a deeper, resonant male voice, stating, "We will bring him hither, and he may believe me." The woman in white tunic then asks, "So, will we go with him all together?" The man in beige tunic, after walking to the right arch, exiting, and re-entering from the left wearing a white hat, rejoins the group and resumes his speech, addressing "Randerson, my friends." He then delivers his "final testament," declaring, "half my good, I will give ye in the way of charity," with the other half to "return to where it once went," maintaining the solemn and dramatic tone of the performance.

The scene unfolds on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. A group of performers in period-style costumes stands in a line across the stage, facing the audience. A man in a brown robe and white hat kneels before a seated woman in a black robe, holding a staff and speaking expressively with a dramatic, theatrical voice, declaring, "do so in spite of the fiend of hell and to abide purgatory that sharp peril." The seated woman, holding a book or tablet, listens intently before responding with a theatrical voice of her own, advising, "Everyman, hearken what I will say. Go to the priesthood, you I advise, and receive of him in any wise, the holy sacrament, anointment together, then shortly see, turn again heaven. We shall all abide you here." During this exchange, the central figure, a man in a beige tunic, speaks expressively and gestures with his hands, bringing them together in a prayer-like motion, then walks to the right arch, exits, and re-enters from the left wearing a white hat, rejoining the group. To his left, a woman in a white tunic with a red vest and brown pants holds a long staff and turns to interact with another woman in a black robe who stands behind the central man, holding a book and observing solemnly. On the far right, a man in a red tunic observes, occasionally gesturing. A woman in a dark dress with a red sash and cross necklace stands to the left, holding a small object and gesturing toward the kneeling man, then stepping back and exiting as he rises. From approximately 0:30, instrumental music, reminiscent of a lute or similar string instrument, plays softly in the background. The kneeling man, still gesturing expressively, resumes speaking with a dramatic tone, saying, "Now Jason, be our altar's feet, now have I true on the whole..." The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person holding up a phone to record, as the consistent lighting enhances the dramatic and solemn tone of the scene.

The scene unfolds on a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance as an attentive audience watches, with one person recording. Center stage, the barefoot Man in Beige Tunic, wearing a white hat and holding a staff, speaks expressively, declaring, "host. Now, friends, I, a pilgrimage, must continue to take. Come with me, thither I will go forth." He gestures with his hands, bringing them together in a prayer-like motion, flanked by the solemn Woman in Black Robe (Standing) and the Woman in White Tunic with her long staff. A stringed instrument plays medieval-style music as the Man in Beige Tunic moves between the arches, exits briefly, and re-enters from the left, rejoining the group. The Man in Red Tunic observes, while the Woman in Brown Cloak subtly engages. The Man in Brown Robe kneels before the barefoot Woman in Black Robe (Seated), holding a staff and speaking with reverence, stating, "Every man, we will not from thee go till thou have gone this voyage long." The Woman in Dark Dress with Red Sash steps forward, gestures toward the kneeling man, then steps back and exits as he rises. The Woman in Black Robe (Seated), holding a book, listens intently and responds with subtle hand gestures, then speaks, "I, discretion, will abide by you also. And though this pilgrimage be never so strong, I will never part you fro. Every man, I will be as sure by thee, as ever I was by Judas Maccabee." The group reassembles, with the Man in Beige Tunic at the center, continuing his expressive dialogue, while the others stand around him, engaged in the unfolding drama, all highlighted by consistent lighting against the dark backdrop, emphasizing the solemn and dramatic tone.

The scene unfolds on a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross on the left arch, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. Center stage, a man in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, and a brown cloak over his shoulders, barefoot and holding a staff, speaks expressively with a dramatic male voice, lamenting, "Alas, I'm so faint, I may not stand. My limbs under me do fold." He continues, declaring, "Let us not return back to this land. Not for all the world's gold. For into this cave must I creep." To his left, a woman in a long black robe over a white tunic, barefoot, seated on a black box, holding a book or tablet, listens intently and responds with a solemn female voice, "And turn to the earth, and there to sleep." The man in beige tunic exclaims in disbelief, "Alas, what? Into this grave?" to which the woman replies, "Yea, there shall you consume, poor wretch." He questions further, "What? As I to wither here? Yet to remain and live on this earth, I may not. But before God Almighty in heaven above." The woman then declares, "I cross out all this. Adieu, by Saint John. I take my cap in my lap, and I'm gone." The man in beige tunic, gesturing with his hands and occasionally bringing them together in a prayer-like motion, asks, "What? Beauty, wither you will forsake me?" The woman concludes with a resolute tone, "Peace. I am dead. I look not behind me. Not in now will give me all the gold in my chest." Throughout this dramatic exchange, to the central figure's right, a woman in a white tunic with a red vest and brown pants, barefoot and holding a long staff, stands beside a man in a red tunic, green vest, and red cap, both observing. Further right, a woman in a long red dress with a white belt, barefoot, stands beside a young man in a beige tunic and brown pants, barefoot, who drinks from a dark mug while engaging in animated conversation with the central figure. On the far right, a man in a brown robe and white hat, barefoot, holds a lute and observes the group. The audience, seated in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person recording on a phone, as the solemn and dramatic atmosphere of the religious or moral play unfolds. The lighting consistently highlights the performers against the dark backdrop, and the central figure moves slightly, gesturing as he speaks, while the woman in the red dress turns and gestures toward the group, adding to the dynamic interaction. The woman in the white tunic with the staff turns to interact with the seated woman in the black robe, who maintains her solemn presence throughout the scene.

On a stage set with three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval or religious ambiance, the Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a beige tunic and brown cloak, stands center stage. He speaks expressively with a distressed tone, lamenting, "Oh, to whom shall I trust? Beauty had me high when she had promised to live with me and die," bringing his hands together in a prayer-like motion. The Woman in Black Robe (Standing) observes solemnly beside him. To the right, the Man in Red Tunic, in a red tunic and holding a long spear, sternly declares, "Everyman, I shall thee also forsake. This game liketh me not at all," gesturing toward the Man in Beige Tunic. The Man in Beige Tunic, sounding desperate, questions, "What? Strength, will he all forsake? Strength, just a little space, tarry," as he kneels briefly before rising again, while the Woman in Black Robe steps forward slightly and gestures subtly. The Man in Red Tunic, resolute, responds, "Nay, by the cross of grace, I hide me from thee fast, though thou weak till thy heart grasp." The Man in Beige Tunic pleads, "He promised otherwise," as the Woman in Dark Dress with Red Sash, holding a small object, gestures toward him before stepping back and exiting the stage. The Man in Red Tunic then dismissively states, "He be old enough, I see, your pilgrimage to take on hand. I repent me hither I came." The Man in Beige Tunic, with a tone of resignation, asks, "You too displease? I am to blame," then asserts, "Yet promise is debt. Yet well willed." The Man in Red Tunic harshly replies, "As for that, I cannot. Thou art but a fool to complain. Thou spendest thy speech and twicest thy brain. Go, thrust thee into the ground," reinforcing the tension. The Woman in White Tunic stands nearby, occasionally turning to interact, while the Man in Brown Robe observes from the far right, holding a lute. The audience watches attentively, with one person recording on a phone, as the dramatic and contemplative scene unfolds.

The scene unfolds on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval or religious ambiance, underscored by melancholic plucked string lute music. Center stage, the Man in Beige Tunic, barefoot and expressively gesturing, speaks with a clear male voice, lamenting, "Both beauty and strength doth fly away fast," and questioning, "Who else but to God shall I now trust?" To his right, the Woman in White Tunic, barefoot and holding a staff, responds with a resigned female voice, stating, "Every man, I will after strength be gone. As for me, I will leave you alone." The Man in Beige Tunic then pleads, "Whether will ye, discretion, will ye forsake me?" The Woman in White Tunic, interacting subtly with the solemn Woman in Black Robe (Standing) who holds a book, firmly replies, "Yea, in faith, for when strength is gone before, I follow after evermore." The Man in Beige Tunic, bringing his hands together in a prayer-like motion, desperately asks, "But the love of all the holy Trinity, will you once look into my grave piteously?" The Woman in White Tunic, now stepping back, delivers a resolute farewell: "Nay, so now I will not come. Now farewell, fellows, everyone." As she steps back, the Woman in Dark Dress with Red Sash, who had gestured towards the Man in Beige Tunic, exits the stage. Brief melancholic lute music plays again. The Man in Beige Tunic, having knelt briefly and risen, continues his lament with a sorrowful tone: "Alas, now discretion doth leave. Discretion, strength, beauty, they all promised to abide by me, yet when death bloweth his blast, fast away do they flee." The Man in Brown Robe, who had been observing with his lute, steps forward, and begins to speak with "Every..." as the segment ends. Throughout, the audience watches attentively, with one person recording on a phone, as the consistent lighting highlights the dramatic and solemn interaction.

The scene unfolds on a stage framed by three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross on the left, and a lantern on the right, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. Center stage, the Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a beige tunic, light pants, brown cloak, and white hat, stands barefoot and speaks expressively, gesturing with his hands and occasionally bringing them together in a prayer-like motion. He laments, "The viper's sake, I followed the other in, finally my tank. Yet, I may well weep, for I considered you to be my best friend. I will keep the name the I more. Farewell forever, nevermore." To his left, the Woman in Black Robe (Standing), dressed in a black robe over a white tunic and barefoot, holds a book and listens intently, occasionally gesturing subtly. To the far left, the Woman in Dark Dress with Red Sash, in a dark dress with a red sash and cross necklace, holds a small object and gestures toward the Man in Beige Tunic, then steps back and exits the stage as he rises. On the far right, the Man in Brown Robe, in a brown robe and white hat, holds a lute and observes the group, remaining mostly still, occasionally providing soft, sustained notes on his instrument. The Man in Beige Tunic continues, "Now fellowship hath forsaken me. There is no one else I can trust, save perhaps my good deeds. Will ye forsake me?" The Woman in Black Robe (Standing) steps forward slightly and responds, identifying herself as 'Good Deeds', "Nay, every man, I will by thy side. I shall not forsake thee indeed. Thou shalt find me a good friend at need." The Man in Beige Tunic acknowledges her, "Gramercy, good deeds." He then kneels briefly before rising again, turning to the Woman in Black Robe (Seated), also in a black robe over a white tunic and barefoot, who is seated on a black box, holding a book or tablet, listening with a solemn expression and responding with subtle hand gestures. He asks her, "Knowledge, will ye forsake me?" She replies, identifying herself as 'Knowledge', "Yay, when ye to your death shall come, but not yet, for no manner of danger." The Man in Beige Tunic expresses gratitude, "Gramercy knowledge, all my heart." The Woman in Black Robe (Seated) reaffirms, "Nay, yet I shall not from hence depart, till I see where ye shall become." The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person holding up a phone to record, as the dramatic interaction unfolds under consistent stage lighting.

The scene unfolds on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic-style arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross, and a lantern, creating a medieval or religious ambiance. Center stage, the Man in Beige Tunic, dressed in a beige tunic, light-colored pants, a brown cloak, and a white hat, stands barefoot, speaking expressively and gesturing with his hands, occasionally bringing them together in a prayer-like motion. He dramatically asks, "Is it time to leave for deeds to depart?" To his left, the Woman in Black Robe (Standing), in a black robe over a white tunic and barefoot, holds a book and listens intently, responding with a clear, measured voice, "Yea, every man. All earthly things is but vanity. Beauty, strength, and discretion do man forsake. Foolish friends and kinsmen that fair speak, all flee and save good deeds, and that am I." The Man in Beige Tunic then expresses resignation, stating, "Me thinketh alas, that I must be gone," before addressing others, "Hear ye all ye who walked and see, for I did forsake my good deeds when I trust have had in her," trailing off with "I believe now I shall." The Woman in Black Robe (Standing) reassures him, "Fear not, I shall speak for thee." The Man in Beige Tunic kneels briefly before rising, pleading, "Have mercy on me, God Almighty, and Mother Maid Mary." On the far right, the Woman in Black Robe (Seated), similarly attired and barefoot, sits on a black box, holding a book or tablet, and responds with a distinct, solemn voice, "Short her end and minister our pain, let us go and never come again." The Man in Beige Tunic then cries out, "Here I cry thee, mercy!" The audience, visible in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person recording on a phone, as the consistent lighting focuses on the performers and the backdrop remains unchanged, contributing to the dramatic and contemplative tone of the scene.

On a stage illuminated by three gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, creating a solemn, medieval ambiance, a deep, male liturgical chanting begins and continues throughout the minute. The Man in Beige Tunic, initially in a beige tunic and brown cloak, interacts briefly with the Woman in Black Robe (Standing), who holds a book. He then removes his cloak, revealing a white tunic with a black belt, and clasps his hands in prayer. As he kneels, he speaks with a clear, resonant voice, against the backdrop of the chanting, declaring, "In thy hands, my soul, I commend. Receive it, Lord, that it be not lost. To save me from the fiend's boast. That I may be with that blessed host, that shall be saved by the day of doom." The Woman in Black Robe (Standing) observes him solemnly as he continues, transitioning to Latin: "In manus tuas, of might most, forever commendo spiritum meum." Maintaining his prayerful pose, he collapses forward onto the stage floor, lying motionless. The Woman in Black Robe (Standing) steps back slightly, while the Woman in Black Robe (Seated), who had been observing from a black box, rises and moves toward the fallen man. The audience, visible in the foreground, watches intently, with one person recording the dramatic and spiritual performance on a phone, all underscored by the continuous, solemn chanting.

The scene unfolds on a stage featuring three illuminated gothic arches with ivy, a cross, and a lantern, establishing a solemn, medieval ambiance, while deep, resonant, Gregorian-like male chanting provides a continuous background. On the left, a woman in a brown cloak and white hat, holding a book, recites in a clear, articulate voice, "Now, hath he suffered that which we all shall endure. Though good deeds shall make all sure." In the center, a man lies motionless on a raised platform, dressed in a white tunic and beige pants, barefoot, appearing deceased. A woman in a black robe approaches him from the right, drapes a white cloth over his body in a symbolic act of mourning, then retrieves a scythe. As she holds the scythe upright in the center, the woman in the brown cloak steps forward, placing her hand on its handle, continuing her recitation: "Now, hath he made ending, me thinketh I hear angels sing. And make great joy and melody, where every man's soul shall received be." She further proclaims, "Now come, excellent, let's suppose to Jesse, you're about thou shalt go. Now that thy soul has taken thy body pro, thy reckoning is crystal clear. And thou shalt into the heavenly sphere, unto which all ye that live well, shalt go after the day of doom." The audience watches intently, with one person recording, as the dramatic and ritualistic performance is emphasized by the focused stage lighting and the evocative, continuous chant.

event_blocks.summary: A barefoot woman in a black robe over a white tunic, holding a book, delivers a solemn address from a raised stage with a backdrop of three illuminated gothic arches adorned with ivy, a cross, and a lantern. She advises the audience, "This memory may all man have in mind. Ye hearers, take it of worth, old and young. And forsake pride for he deceiveth you in the end. And remember, beauty, five wits, strength, and discretion. They all at last do every man forsake. Save his good deeds, them there he doth take. But beware for, and may be small, before God, he hath no help at all. There too help the Trinity. Amen, say ye, for Saint Charity," gesturing expressively before exiting stage right. Instrumental music, featuring a lute-like string instrument, begins as a woman in a dark dress with a red sash, a man in a beige tunic, a woman in a white tunic, and a man in a red tunic holding a spear enter and converge center stage, where the woman in the dark dress gestures towards the kneeling man in the beige tunic. The audience applauds enthusiastically, with one member recording on a phone. As applause fades, solemn, melancholic choral music with male and female voices and a string instrument plays, accompanying black screens with white text credits for cast, musicians, crew, and special thanks, including roles like 'Everyman,' 'God/Confession,' and 'Death,' and credits for lute, flute, vocals, and percussion. The segment concludes with the 'EVERYMAN' logo, featuring a scallop shell and red cross, displayed against the continuing choral music. The credits then scroll upward on a black screen with white text, listing production roles, set and costume design, makeup, and special thanks to individuals and organizations like Dr. Chris Nighman, Professor Alicia McKenzie, the WLU Department of History, and the Wilfrid Laurier University Students Union, along with club partners and funding sources, all while a solemn, multi-voice choral piece reminiscent of a Gregorian chant plays. The screen transitions to a static white background featuring the 'Laurier Historical Theatre Society' (LHTS) logo, with stylized purple letters 'LHTS' and a classical column, and the full name in gold script below, before fading to black.

Holding a book or tablet, she delivers a solemn address in a clear, formal voice, stating, "This memory may all man have in mind. Ye hearers, take it of worth, old and young. And forsake pride for he deceiveth you in the end. And remember, beauty, five wits, strength, and discretion. They all at last do every man forsake. Save his good deeds, them there he doth take. But beware for, and may be small, before God, he hath no help at all. There too help the Trinity. Amen, say ye, for Saint Charity." She gestures expressively, occasionally looking down at her book, against a backdrop of three illuminated gothic-style arches adorned with green ivy, a small cross on the left arch, and a lantern on the right, creating a religious or medieval ambiance. The audience, seated in the foreground, watches attentively, with one person holding up a phone to record. After her address, she turns and walks off stage to the right, exiting the scene. Following a brief pause, instrumental music, featuring a melodic string instrument reminiscent of a lute, begins to play as a group of performers enters from both sides. This group includes a woman in a dark dress with a red sash and cross necklace, a man in a beige tunic, a woman in a white tunic with a black belt, and a man in a red tunic holding a spear. They converge near the center stage, engaging in what appears to be a dramatic interaction, with the woman in the dark dress gesturing toward the man in the beige tunic, who is now kneeling. The lighting remains consistent, focused on the stage, emphasizing the performers and the symbolic backdrop, before the audience erupts in applause.
